message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Older adults with coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) face an increased risk of adverse health outcomes including mortality. Ethical guidelines consider allocation of limited resources based on likelihood of survival, frilty, co-morbidities and age. However, the association of frailty with clinical outcomes in older COVID-19 patients remains unclear.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To determine the association between frailty and short-term mortality in older adults hospitalized for COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Retrospective single-center observational study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting and participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>N = 81 patients with COVID-19 confirmed by reverse-transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR), at the Geriatrics department of Imelda general hospital, Belgium.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Measurements</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Frailty was graded according to the Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS). Demographic, biochemical and radiological variables, co-morbidities, symptoms and treatment were extracted from electronic medical records.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Participants (N = 48 women, 59%) had a median age of 85 years (range 65-97 years), median CFS score of 7 (range 2 - 9), and 42 (52%) were long-term care residents. Within six weeks, eighteen patients died. Mortality was significantly but weakly associated with age (Spearman r = 0.241,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    = 0.03) and CFS score (r = 0.282,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    = 0.011), baseline lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) (r = 0.301,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    = 0.009), lymphocyte count (r = -0.262,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    = 0.02) and RT-PCR Ct value (r = -0.285,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    = 0.015). Mortality was not associated with long-term care residence, dementia, delirium or polypharmacy. In multivariable logistic regression analyses, CFS, LDH and RT-PCR Ct values (but not age) remained independently associated with mortality. Both age and frailty had poor specificity to predict survival. A multivariable model combining age, CFS, LDH and viral load significantly predicted survival.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and implications</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Although their prognosis is worse, even the oldest and most severely frail patients may benefit from hospitalization for COVID-19, if sufficient resources are available.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>BRIEF SUMMARY</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Outcomes of frail older adults hospitalized for COVID-19, particularly long-term care residents, remain unclear. In this retrospective cohort, frailty predicted mortality independently of age or established biomarkers.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Frailty and mortality in hospitalized older adults with COVID-19: retrospective observational study
